1: A theme: Psalm 55 is a Maskil (an instructional poem) of David, beginning with the raw plea "Give ear to my prayer, O God; do not hide yourself from my plea for mercy." The hidden God who does not answer is the deepest fear in the Psalter's lament tradition (cf. Psalms 10:1; Psalms 22:1; Psalms 44:24). Christ himself prays this petition in Gethsemane: "Abba, Father, everything is possible for you. Take this cup from me" (Mark 14:36). The cry that God not hide himself is the prayer that the cross appears to negate ("My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" Matthew 27:46) but the resurrection finally answers.
2: A theme: "Pay attention to me and answer me; I am troubled in my lament and I am agitated." The agitation (ʾārîd bĕśîḥî, I wander/moan in my concern) of the Psalmist in crisis is the emotional register of Gethsemane: "My soul is overwhelmed with sorrow to the point of death" (Matthew 26:38). Christ enters the full reality of human anguish — not performing calm — and the prayer to be heard and answered is the prayer the Father answers not by removing the cup but by raising Christ through death.
3: A shadow: "because of the threatening voice of the enemy, the oppressive presence of the wicked; they bring evil upon me, and in anger they press hard against me." The threatening pressure of the enemy — voices and presence pressing hard — is the Passion narrative's texture. John 11:53 ("they plotted to take his life"), Matthew 26:3-5 (the Sanhedrin's deliberate plan), and the crowd's escalating pressure on Pilate (John 19:12-15: "Crucify him!") are the fulfillment of the hostile pressing of Psalms 55:3.
4: A shadow: "My heart is writhing in anguish within me; the terrors of death have fallen on me." The terrors of death (ʾêmôt māwet) that descend on the Psalmist are the Passion's emotional reality. Acts 2:24 states that God "raised him up, loosing the pangs of death (lysas tas ōdinas tou thanatou) because it was impossible for death to hold him." The "pangs" / "terrors" of death fell on Christ at Gethsemane and the cross — and the resurrection is the divine loosing of those very terrors.
5: A shadow: "Fear and trembling have overtaken me, and shuddering has covered me over." The triple collapse of composure — fear, trembling, shuddering — is the extreme of human vulnerability before mortal threat. In Gethsemane, Luke 22:44 records: "And being in anguish, he prayed more earnestly, and his sweat was like drops of blood falling to the ground." The shuddering of Psalms 55:5 is enacted literally in the Agony in the Garden — the one who is the source of all courage entered the full experience of human terror before death.
6: A revelation of God: "And I said, 'If only I had wings like a dove! I would fly away and find rest.'" The wish for escape — dove's wings to flee suffering — is the honest prayer that did not override duty. Christ did not escape: "Father, if you are willing, take this cup from me; yet not my will, but yours be done" (Luke 22:42). The dove-wing escape of Psalms 55:6 is the human desire that the incarnate Son also felt, and which he voluntarily set aside. The Spirit descends as a dove at his baptism (Matthew 3:16) — the dove that stays, rather than flees, empowering the mission.
7: A theme: "Yes, I would flee far away; I would make my dwelling in the wilderness." The wilderness as refuge — distance from human violence — is the pattern Christ actually entered at the beginning of his ministry (Matthew 4:1: "Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness"). But where Psalms 55:7 wishes for escape to the wilderness, Christ's wilderness is not escape but preparation for conflict. He emerges from the wilderness to face the very opposition Psalm 55 describes — he chose engagement over escape.
8: A theme: "I would hurry to my refuge from the driving wind and the storm." The shelter from storm is the image of YHWH as refuge (Psalms 46:1; Isaiah 25:4: "a shelter from the storm"). In the NT, Christ is the shelter who calms the storm itself (Mark 4:39: "Quiet! Be still!") — not merely a refuge from the storm but the one who ends the storm. The desperate flight to shelter in Psalms 55:8 becomes, in Christ, the arrival at the one who has already stilled the chaos that threatens.
9: A shadow: "Confuse them, O Lord, split their tongues apart; for I see violence and contention in the city." The prayer for the confusion of violent, divisive speech in the city recalls Babel (Genesis 11:7-9: "Come, let us go down and confuse their language") and echoes the judgment on Babylon of Revelation 18. The "city" of Psalms 55:9 racked by violence is Jerusalem in its corrupt state — and it is in Jerusalem that Christ is rejected and killed. The judgment on the city that opposed the Messiah comes in 70 AD (Matthew 24:2).
10: A shadow: "Day and night they patrol its walls; iniquity and trouble are entrenched within it." The city fortified by iniquity — the watchers who enforce the corrupt order — is the Jerusalem of the Passion narrative. The chief priests, scribes, and elders (Matthew 26:3) who plan against Jesus within the holy city represent the iniquity "entrenched" at the center of what should be the place of righteous rule. The city that should shelter the righteous becomes the place where the Righteous One is killed.
11: A shadow: "Ruin is entrenched at its center; oppression and fraud never leave its public spaces." The corruption of the city's center — the marketplace of the heart of the covenant community — is what Christ confronts directly in the temple cleansing (John 2:13-17; Matthew 21:12-13). "My house will be called a house of prayer, but you are making it a den of robbers" (Matthew 21:13): the "fraud" that "never leaves its public spaces" in Psalms 55:11 is the commercial exploitation that Christ drives out of the temple courts.
12: A type: "For it is not an enemy who insults me — that I could endure; nor is it someone who hates me who has risen up against me — from that I could hide." The manageable grief of an enemy's opposition — bearable, even hideable from — establishes the contrast with what follows. This verse frames the specific quality of Judas's betrayal of Christ: it was not the opposition of Rome or the Sanhedrin that wounds most deeply, but the betrayal by one of the Twelve. The Psalm's structure of "what I could endure" vs. "what I cannot" mirrors the Gospel's framing of Judas as the deepest wound.
13: A type: "But it is you — a person like me, my peer, my companion, the one I confided in." The identity of the betrayer as intimate peer and confidant — "my peer (kĕʿerĕkî), my companion (ʾallûpî), the one I confided in (mĕyuddāʿî)" — is the precise profile of Judas Iscariot. John 13:18 quotes Psalms 41:9 in the betrayal context ("He who shared my bread has turned against me"), but Psalms 55:13 provides the fuller emotional portrait. Luke 6:16 simply notes "Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor" — the one who had been confided in fully.
14: A type: "We shared deep, sweet conversation together; together we walked in God's house among the festival crowd." The shared worship and intimate dialogue — the table fellowship of Psalms 55:14 — is precisely the context of the Last Supper where the betrayal is announced (John 13:21-30). The "festival crowd" in God's house is the Passover setting: together they reclined at table for the covenant meal, and it is Judas who leaves that meal to betray the one who had shared everything with him. The intimacy of Psalms 55:14 is the Last Supper's intimacy betrayed.
15: A shadow: "Let death ambush them; let them go down to Sheol alive; for wickedness lives in their dwellings and among them." The imprecatory prayer for the sudden death of the betrayers and violent enemies is the justice cry of the Psalmist. Acts 1:18 records the judgment on Judas: "he fell headlong, his body burst open and all his intestines spilled out." The "going down to Sheol" language of Psalms 55:15 is a type of the eschatological judgment on those who betray covenant relationship — the judgment is already enacted in miniature in Judas's end.
16: A revelation of God: "As for me, I will call upon God, and the LORD will rescue me." The personal declaration of trust contrasted with the enemies' fate is the pivot of Psalm 55. This is the pattern of Gethsemane → Easter: Christ calls upon God in prayer ("Father, if you are willing, take this cup from me," Luke 22:42) and the LORD rescues him — not by removing the cup but by raising him from the dead. The rescue of Psalms 55:16 is the resurrection, which comes through the very crisis that produces the prayer.
17: A revelation of God: "Evening and morning and at noon I cry out my lament and sigh; and he hears my voice." The threefold daily prayer — evening, morning, noon — is the pattern of constant access to YHWH (cf. Daniel's thrice-daily prayer, Daniel 6:10). The NT promise is even broader: "pray continually" (1 Thessalonians 5:17). Christ as the great high priest "always lives to make intercession" for his people (Hebrews 7:25) — the continuous intercession that Psalms 55:17's rhythm points toward. The "he hears my voice" is the confidence that resurrection grounds.
18: A revelation of God: "He ransoms me safely from the battle I am in, for they are many who oppose me." The ransom from the hostile many (pādāh bĕšālôm napšî miqĕrāb-lî, he redeemed my life in peace from the battle against me) is the covenant vocabulary of the Exodus. Christ's ransom is the ultimate redemption: Mark 10:45, "the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many." The "many who oppose" in Psalms 55:18 become the "many" for whom the ransom is paid in Mark 10:45.
19: A revelation of God: "God will hear and afflict them, the one who is enthroned from of old — because they show no sign of change and have no fear of God." The divine response to the unrepentant — hearing, afflicting — is grounded in the eternal enthronement of YHWH. "No fear of God" (ʾên ḥălîpôt lāhemô wĕlōʾ yārĕʾû ʾĕlōhîm) is cited by Paul in Romans 3:18 as the culminating indictment of universal human sinfulness: "There is no fear of God before their eyes." The lack of repentance and God-fear that Psalms 55:19 condemns is the condition Paul declares universal — and which Christ addresses in the atonement.
20: A shadow: "He raised his hand against those who were at peace with him; he broke his covenant." The covenant-breaking by the one who was at peace — lifting the hand against friends, violating the solemn bond — is the deepest form of treachery in the ancient world. The betrayal of Jesus by Judas (who had received the dipped bread, the final sign of table-fellowship, John 13:26-27) is this covenant-breaking at its most extreme. Matthew 26:50: "Friend, do what you came for" — Jesus addresses the covenant-breaker as "friend" even in the act of betrayal.
21: A type: "His words were smoother than butter, yet war was in his heart; his speech was softer than oil, but it was all drawn swords." The gap between smooth speech and violent intent — honeyed words concealing weaponized intent — is the profile of hypocrisy that Christ repeatedly confronts. Matthew 26:48-49: Judas had arranged a signal — "The one I kiss is the man; arrest him." "Going at once to Jesus, Judas said, 'Greetings, Rabbi!' and kissed him." The kiss is the "smooth as butter" speech of Psalms 55:21 — and it is the signal for arrest.
22: A direct citation: "Throw your burden onto the LORD, and he will hold you up; he will never let the righteous be shaken." 1 Peter 5:7 quotes this verse in the context of community care under suffering: "Cast all your anxiety on him because he cares for you." The Psalm's command to throw (šĕlēk ʿal-YHWH yĕhābĕkā) the burden onto YHWH is the OT grounding for the NT's call to anxiety-free trust in God's care. Christ is the mediator through whom the casting of burdens reaches the Father — Matthew 11:28: "Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest."
23: A revelation of God: "But you, O God, will bring them down into the pit of ruin; men of bloodshed and treachery will not reach half their days. But I — I will trust in you." The final declaration of personal trust despite the enemies' violence is the Psalm's resolution. The emphatic "But I — I (waʾănî)" stands against the enemies' fate. Hebrews 2:13 applies the trust-language of the Psalms to Christ: "I will put my trust in him" (citing Isaiah 8:17). The one who trusts in Psalms 55:23 is the type of Christ who "entrusted himself to him who judges justly" (1 Peter 2:23) through the entire Passion.
